speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This interview with Nellie Wood Smith took place at her home at Bovill on  September 10th 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.  Do you remember much of what Bovill was like in the very first days that you were  here? What were the first impressions you had?  Well, I think about the first thing, do I remember that? I said, what's that building?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, that's the depot. So, you know, the road used to come in, and it's coming right  straight up to the depot. And then, you know, the road used to come in, and it's coming  straight up this hill from Main Street, or from this street, I mean, straight up the  hill across the track. It used to make a turn up there just not far from the bridge, about  even with that cedar mill, you know, across the road there. The road used to turn to the  left and go over almost those big barns. And then it came right straight down Main Street,  coming into town.  The road has been changed since then. And when it came into, when it came across the  middle, you see, the first good look we got at what's supposed to be the town was they  were just building the depot. And there was, oh, there was a barber shop here, and there  was a men's clothing store, almost. It was kind of a little bit of everything, but it  was mostly the lumberjacks' supplies, mostly. They had their logging boots and things of  that sort, and the pin pants. And Mr. Parker had this, E.K. Parker from Princeton had his  store, that men's clothing. And then A.A. Anderson was... he was from Tennessee, and  he was running the barber shop and, well, there was a restaurant, I think the restaurant  was in work and order at that time. He was one of the early ones, anyway. I don't know  for sure, but in the name of Lester Smith. He was running the restaurant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: School there in 1907.  Here?  Yes, in Bovill.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, it was a room a little bigger than, well maybe as long as this area into the kitchen, too.  And it was one room, and it wasn't any wider than this. That was our school.  And we had the lady teacher. Her name was, Mame Rodner. She was a Moscow girl.  And she died just a few years ago. I went to see her when she was in the hospital.  And so, but that at the time was,  . . .  Well, oh I don't know then. It might have been 18 or 20 children here at that time.  But the school building was right over there at the foot of that hill, where it starts up.  And the people over there by the name of the Malborees, they lived there for years now.  They, the people that lived there before that.  And they lived there, too. Yes, and those people you want to know about, there's a fellow's here by the name of, of Hughes and Shattuck.  No, that's another Hughes.  Shattuck and Hughes, I've heard. And they had a meat market.  And Mr., I think Shattuck bought our schoolhouse from there, and moved it over here, kind of by, this way a little ways, and behind the schoolhouse.  Where the schoolhouse presently is.  And, and so this teacher was, was Mame Rodner, and that's the kid that she had, had charge of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the school like compared to going to school in Troy?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: It didn't compare.  I, I hate to have these things go down in record, because it, but we,  Well, the truth is the truth.  But, I was going to say that she didn't have this, growing on over, she was a new teacher, but she didn't have any control of the kids.  And, and she just, she wasn't cut out for school nature, I guess.  Because, well, we all did as we pleased, mostly.  And, the kids played around, and if she told them to do something they didn't want to, why they didn't do it.  And so, you know, you know what you learn in, in a school like that.  Not very much, you know, no ministry, probably.  And, anyway, in the long run, I, and then, I was taking eighth grade that time.  I had been in, taking sixth grade at Troy.  And, well, I was doing eighth grade work with the eighth grade, too.  I was taking a mixed sixth and eighth.  And, there wasn't anybody in the seventh.  So, I, I had, came there with the intention of being in sixth grade.  But, when I got to it, I was part sixth and part eighth.  And, when I came up here then, I started the eighth grade.  And, went through that first year.  And, the end of the year, you know, they used to send out their, their examination papers.  Their questions, all came from the, from Bonner's Ferry.  From the, from the state superintendent.  You had to take that exam from the, from the state, or the county, which was the state, I think it was, at that time.  And, so, they, when the, when the exam, when you took the examination, there was not one of the, of the class that passed.  So, you can see what they learned.  And, I was always way ahead of every, of the things there.  And, I didn't know where else they had good schools.  You may have learned at Troy, just before that, you didn't play around.  Boy, your note was, notes was right in your book.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you not pass it either?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Not up here at all. I didn't pass it. Didn't know nothing.  Were, were you real disappointed about that?  Disappointed? I was disgusted.  Because, I knew that I could do better than that.  But, but, the kids all played, and, well, I fell in line.  And, I could, well, I didn't do so much, but we just didn't study.  But, there's some of the,  the kids, there was a couple of the girls of the,  Pets, of the teachers, she'd gotten a week or two start.  Then, well, then, no, she'd gotten a couple months of kids.  We didn't get it here until October, the end of October.  But, she had, she just thought those girls were the,  the cleverest things that she'd found.  Oh, they just, they're, they're wonderful.  And, the best little spellers, she just, they just,  I just, it was just shocker, you know, that they were so,  and I got, I kind of got on the wall cross with her,  right off the bat.  Because, she was raving about those girls.  And, my sister and I were just about the same ages  as those two girls that she was talking about.  And, I told her, I said, well, I like spelling, too, very much.  And, I said that I had, well, I said, in fact, I just got through,  the, the, of course, the teacher there had let me out  because I was ready for passing anyhow, you know.  I let me out the first, well, the other month of school  I was supposed to have had down there before I left.  But, he gave me my pass and he said that I,  I was fully qualified.  And, so he.  But, she was playing favorites.  Yes, but, so when she, when it got through to her,  she was, started bragging then.  I said, I just, just finished school down there.  And, I said, the teacher offered a prize for the one,  in the spelling contest.  He'd have, about every other Friday, he'd have spelling.  And, then the odd Friday in between,  he'd have some sort of entertainment or games  or something like that.  And, so he offered a prize for the one that had the best spelling.  He gave, had the best spelling.  And, he had the head marks for, over the,  the whole term for the head marks for spelling.  The one with the head at the end of the, the, Dave,  he got the head mark for that time.  And, well, there was another boy and I that got the,  that were running the hot race.  The rest didn't, didn't quite keep up,  but they just tit for tat, he and I.  And, then I think when it got, got to the end,  he liked me pretty well anyway.  I think we were, we were rather mutual.  I'm a fine kid.  And, so I think he politely kind of missed a word maybe  to let me have it the last day.  Because we just, it was always first one day then the next.  So, I don't know whether he did that or not,  but I always thought maybe he did to favor me.  But, he, he was awfully good.  But, I told him, I just got through this,  I told this teacher I just got through this.  And, I said I had won the spelling contest there  three years in succession.  And, I thought, I thought I just wanted to hit it hard  and then let her have it.  I was so vexed at her for bragging.  I thought, wait, let her show, show what they can do.  But, well, all right, that would be a good idea.  She said, she just, she would give the prize too,  she said, for the head marks.  And, she was going to put me in my place, I guess.  So, that pleased me too.  And, so we started doing that.  I came home with the prize that way.  who was going to be there in a year or two,  over her, over her pets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, but.  Had you missed the year of school when you were up in the homestead?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I think the homestead, hm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you regret it, that, that not going that year?  Or did you, was there enough to compensate for it that you didn't mind?  Oh, you mean after we got here?  No, when you were up there.  Oh, well, we didn't, we just kind of,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: we were there, just kind of more or less ignored it,  the idea.  We just had that to do, you know,  they had moved out to, to gain the land,  and we didn't make any fuss about it.  But, Mom and Dad were kind of making a fuss about it.  And, they finally said,  well, we just got to get out of here for these kids,  or they won't grow anything pretty soon.  And, they said, we got to get them in school.  So, that's about all there was to it.  Is that the main reason that, that your father decided to move and to go over here?  Mm-hmm. Yeah.  Well, he was working here all the time,  and he'd have to hike then, on the weekends,  he'd hike clear into, to our homestead.  And, I think that was eight miles,  it's six or eight miles, I don't remember which it was,  but I think it was, it was either six miles or eight miles,  due south of Bovill.  And, you see the, the Boise-Meridian runs right through Bovill.  And, it just cuts our homestead in half.  Mm-hmm.  And, it'll down that way, too.  He, he had to hike in from here to get there?  Mm-hmm. He would hike home on the weekends,  and spend, be home for the weekend,  and then he'd back in Monday for the work.  And, he was a carpenter, was working here.  The buildings, the buildings were all going up then, so.  A lot of work.  Well, quite a bit of it, because there wasn't too many carpenters around.  Most of the,  the fellows were working in the woods,  for the logging, it was.  So.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he continue to keep the homestead  when you moved into town here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, he did for a while, but,  you see, you have to live so many months a year  on that homestead in order to claim it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For five years?  Uh-huh.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And, well, he decided rather than to move back and forth,  uh, each year, or something like that,  they decided they would commute that homestead  and turn it to stone and timber.  And, you could pay it off.  You could buy your fish buying your land then,  instead of living on it.  So, that's what they did.  They commuted on the homestead  and turned it into stone and timber,  and then, they didn't have to go back anymore.  But.  And, they sold it after a while.  The Potlatch wanted it for the timber.  And, so, they sold it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he did on that,  as compared to what he had paid to buy it as stone and timber?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I suppose it could be figured out.  I might be able to figure it out,  but, you see, they take that 40 acres,  and they take it.  And, they, how much is it?  They pay so much each year.  And, then, the next year is just that much less.  And, then, it goes down to the last year.  They don't, they just have to, they pay it out.  You see, they've got 40 years.  They just pay it in.  And, that, uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It doesn't really matter.  But, I just wondered if he,  if he really got ahead, you know,  by selling it to Potlatch.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they sold it.  They sold it for cash,  but I don't know how much.  I think he,  I think he only got 1200 or 1000 or something for it.  I don't know how,  I don't remember.  I did know at the time,  but I just wasn't particularly interested,  I guess, in that.  And, so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you know the Bovill?  Oh, yes.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Very well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me what they were like?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, the two Bovill girls were,  were my sister and my,  my chums, especially.  And, they were very nice girls.  And, oh, I was interested,  because they had a lot of things to tell  about their, their travels,  you know, before we met them.  And, well, they were about our same ages.  And, they had been,  the folks had been living here in,  in this settlement for,  I don't know,  I don't know, a year or two,  or two, three years, maybe.  But, the girls had to be in school,  so they would send them to  the girls' school right now in,  in Spokane each year.  I,  it probably doesn't matter what name the school,  but I did know the name of their school,  but I've forgotten now.  I think it was once in a while.  The, the,  the girls had gone to school up there,  and then when they started the school here,  went out to the girls' school here.  And, they were lovely girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what did they have to say about their experiences  before they ever came to Bovill?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they didn't say too much.  They were, they were kept close  in the school they went to over there.  It was just a matter of a different school.  And, and then they come home.  And there's not much excitement,  but their parents, these girls might have been,  I don't know just how many,  but how long they had been going this,  that back and forth.  I don't know how long the Bovill lived here  before they started the town here.  And, but it had been a few years, I know.  And, maybe got longer because they bought it from,  from, well they, I think there's a piece of land  up north a little further here  that they, that they homesteaded too.  And, oh there were several people that were,  there's a lot of people that were taking homesteads  about that time.  They had their, there was a lot of the surveyors  and things on the job, the locators, you know,  that were, that just made that their business.  Some of them came out from Troy and all that.  They'd take the people up and locate them on this,  show them their land, find their numbers  and locate it for them.  And they go to the town.  They go back and file.  And, so anyhow, they were, one of them was,  Hayes lived next to it.  He had taken his homestead and,  right about the same time as ours.  So there's Millers and, Miller and Hayes  and McGarry and Hollenbeck and Phelps.  A lot of them in our group, you know,  kind of neighborhood.  And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the Bovill seem,  he was called Lord Bovill,  did they seem aristocratic to you?  No, not especially.  They were very common.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: They were, you know, you realize they were people  that knew what, knew the way around  and knew what they were doing  and had educated people.  But they didn't make a fuss about it.  They were just very, very common.  She was, and,  she had taken,  well I don't know whether she had taken  special training or not,  but she had studied a lot about,  in her own books and all,  about in doctoring and nursing and such.  Like, she had,  had been,  she,  she got, you know, there was no doctor here  for so many months or years.  There was no doctor to call back on.  And the closest,  closest would have been Troy or Moscow  or somewhere out like that.  And when you got in with six foot of snow  and somebody got hurt or got sick by,  it was quite a problem.  So they,  so it fell to Mrs. Bovill to,  she became the nurse here.  And she was,  when anything happened to anybody,  they generally call on her  and she would give them the instructions.  And tell them what to do or go out on a call  with whoever came in.  And I know,  I was in the,  and she,  they ran the post office,  you know, that time.  They had the post office here.  And Charlotte was her name.  His name was Hugh.  So,  so they,  I know one time that I was in the post office there  to get the mail.  And I was awfully tight in my,  my throat was awfully tight.  And I was just coughing and feeling pretty bummed.  And she said,  look here young lady,  come here a minute,  let me see that throat.  And she,  she said,  you just better go home and get,  go out and doctor that throat  or it's going to wind up in pneumonia.  And so anyhow,  she told me about it.  And she told me then,  I told her,  well I think my mother had been using turpentine  and lard.  And all,  you know,  the grease you get like they did in those days.  But she said that,  that the,  what she had to use for her youngest girl was Gwyn.  And the older was Dorothy.  And so she,  Gwyn was,  was subject to Quincy every so often.  She'd run in and she'd just bring a choke to death.  And so,  but the,  she finally found out that with her,  whenever she got that,  she just,  just picked her peck up.  And she'd put her knife,  she'd put the heavy big bandage of the ice cold packs around her throat.  And then wrapped it in,  in a wool blanket or something.  Put her to bed.  And I don't know what she,  whether she gave her any warm drink of any kind.  I don't remember.  She couldn't swallow hardly anyhow.  And she'd just pile the covers on and make her fall into a sweat.  And she'd throw it out and come out of it.  And so she told me that,  that's what she wanted me to do.  She said I was having pneumonia.  And so I went home and told Mother what she said to do.  And well,  I got pecked into a good cold pack and then the,  the heavy bow went on the outside of it.  And it loosened it up right out,  and put it on my chest and it loosened right up.  But she was,  and then there was so many of the men,  got hurt in the woods and got cut or broken legs or something.  She was,  she was the only nurse around at that time.  So she'd help us whenever it was needed.  And he was,  well he was with the surveyors more.  And he had horses to take care of.  They had,  they all had their riding horses.  And,  well,  they'd go out.  And in the spring,  when there,  when the snow would go out,  and then,  that here that meadow across here,  by this bridge,  all just a lake.  And the,  so they had some boats out there.  And they'd all boat right around.  And that,  and that lake is,  of course,  more or less a resort to them.  So those are the things that,  and I remember hearing the girls talk the most about the living,  used to tell me about,  about,  about, they were connected with the royalty in England, somewhere or other. I don't know  just what the connection was. They told me, but I didn't remember, because it was all  Greek to me. So they told me about their, what is it, their, oh, this, kind of sad signals  or their coat of arms. I thought it was just their coat of arms, and showed me what it  was, and told me about it, but I didn't remember it. But they used to tell me a few things  like that they'd talk about. I don't know whether they came here with the parents when  they first came here, and I think they did come.  Yeah.  Because Gwen's picture, they had a picture of Gwen taken up here at this place when Gwen  was quite small. So I think they lived here several years before they got school started  here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the kids, the girls think of the change in town? The fact that what was once  just a resort came turning into a town.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I think they just enjoyed themselves. They'd go riding once in a while on their  horses, and they would like to tread around through the brush in the woods and just enjoy  it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I read in John Miller's book, or I heard that the Bovill were really rather sad  about the coming of a lumber company to change the town so much. Do you think that's true,  or do you think that they wanted it here?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I don't really know. But I know that they were instrumental in, well, taking, in building  the town as it was going on. And they've had it, they had it incorporated. And I don't  know. And the things they're, they purchased a lot of this land. It's just from the, oh,  I think it was from the Milwaukee Railroad, the WIM Railroad Company or something. It was  all purchased. And I don't know. But I think they, they were really, they were really,  they must have been more or less in favor of it. And they had the town reserved the  right to name the town after themselves. And I don't know. I don't remember, don't know  much about their, their opinion or their desires on that line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, do you know much, did you hear much about the, what the resort had, had, had been

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: before the town came? I've heard that a lot of people came out here.  Yes, it used to be that, that was before my time here. And I didn't know much about it.  But I've heard, I've heard after I came here that they used to, people used to come here,  here, oh, just, just any amount of, you know, they found out that the place was here. And  they would bring some, you know, locator, bring someone in, you know, to look at the  land and they all thought it was kind of a well way out there in the nature's, you know,  home garden. And they decided it'd just be fun to hear or to see. And so they, they would  come back, you know, after they, oh, I know sometime when they had kind of vacation and  all, they just hopped in their sled wagon or whatever it would happen to be and just  up this way and there's quite a few people that used to come up and spend a little time  in the hotel. The Volvo then was running a hotel up there, where their Volvo hotel is  now. But they, I guess, just drove along with you all the time, kind of. But I've heard  quite a lot of them. Then you see up about two miles from here, there are three, there  was Collins. There was people up there, they're homesteading. Most all the Fries had their  homesteads up there, right around that meadow. They kind of got in early, so they got pick  and choice. There's Jake. There's Jake and John and Ed.  All those were Fries. I don't know whether that's all or not, but anyway, in the long  run, they had a little school up their house, up their corner of the woods too then for  a while, up at Collins. The school teacher, they had a little school up there, and they  had a little kid up there, was, I don't think I'm going to tell you, but somebody or other,  and it was the teacher. And as time went on, by, I guess it was Jake, or it was Abe, was  another one of them too. I guess Jake was the first one.  Yeah, Jake was the first one.  And then there was the other one, I mean, that, they were mostly young folks, kind of.  And he married the school mom, and had their home up there. And then, her sister was a  teacher, so, there you go, her and she came and got the school, and kept it up as long  as it, as long as it had the school up there. And I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She didn't marry one of the Fries too?  No.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Oh.  No, I said, no. She was going to follow down on the ridge here. I think, yeah, I think  the easy one is the carpenter's gear, you know.  Hmm.  A whole thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Um, when you were, when you were, how it around together, what did you see?  What was there for you girls to do here when you first came?  Nothing particularly.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Just make your fun as you go, usually, and things of that kind, you know.  And, well, there's always a box in the woods and there's beautiful flowers here that you could gather in the season.  And we didn't do much hiking around, but, you know, take walks out a little ways once in a while.  But my sister and I, well, the folks usually found something for us to do.  It wasn't hard for them to find a job for us.  They had the yard to rake or the dishes to do or something like that quite a lot.  But we could always have time with the girls up there.  And, well, that's what we did.  And they had chores to do, too.  Now, these other two girls that I said turned out to be the teachers' pets, they tried to put, you know, chum in with the global girls, too.  But I don't know.  I don't think they were quite so chummy as...  I don't know.  They were kind of...  I don't know how much you'd say.  They were...  They were kind of, oh, get on with yourself, you know.  You know, just a little bit, I don't know, just a little bit stuck up or something.  And they didn't have anything to be stuck up over, in particular.  They didn't have a very nice disposition.  And their father was running the saloon here.  And so it wasn't anything to brag about anywhere that I could see.  It was just their own way of acting.  And finally, well, when we were going to school, they were so...  Well, I guess the girl got jealous of me after we'd been here a while.  And we began to get acquainted.  And we, well, finally there's something for everybody to do.  And I don't remember...  I don't remember whether the local girls took in any of those parties or not.  But, you see, they were...  Folks kept them in pretty close up there at home.  And then the town had kind of gathered, you know, built out a down in this end of it.  And the people decided, well, they'd get kind of cabin fever.  And they decided they'd get together, you know, for a evening's fun or something.  Well, one evening they'd come down to our place.  And Dad played the violin.  And I had the guitar.  And we had the mandolin, too.  My sister had the mandolin.  And so we'd come down there and we'd play and sing and play games and popcorn and eat apples and pull taffy and such things as that.  And Dad...  That would be our entertainment.  And maybe they'd strike up a tune or two and they'd move things back and the dancers could drill for a square dance.  Or in waltz, a little.  And that was the main part.  But we had a lot more fun than the kids have nowadays than hunting entertainment.  What kind of songs did you sing?  What did you play?  Well, the most popular, you know.  The Red Wing was very popular at that time.  And all the homes and all the old Black Joe and all those old-timer songs.  Mostly of that kind.  And once in a while, we'd set up a hymn.  And it might wind up with a hymn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you sing songs that you came out with from Missouri that your father had...  The folks had learned back there?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, not in particular.  Because I wasn't old enough to sing much, but I remembered some of them.  My granddad, my father's father, would play the violin.  And he had a violin, too, and he would play.  And he'd play some of those...  Hodong kind, I think they were.  The Turkey in the Straw and...  What's that?  Sailor's Hornpipe.  And some of those real fast things.  And some of those had little tunes to them.  And I was singing some of those once in a while.  But that's about all.  But when I got into Wyoming then, in Laramie, I went to...  Got there, I think, in the...  Must have been right around, I think, near time for school.  Or maybe school had already been going.  I don't remember what time of year we were there.  But they'd become acquainted with the Wechters across the street from where we lived.  And Millie Wechter was...  Well, I was just a little kid when we was in there.  And she was, oh, I think about 15, 16 years old, and maybe in high school.  And so Mother asked her if she'd care to take me to school with her.  And I wanted to.  Quite a little...  Several blocks.  And she said, yes, she'd be delighted to go with me.  So I was under her wing for getting started to school.  And so I went to the first...  They put me in the first grade, of course.  I had never...  I wasn't in school much.  I had gone to school in Missouri with my aunt.  It was still in school.  But she was 17, maybe 16.  And they'd take me to the school with them in Missouri there once in a while.  And the teacher would...  I had learned a lot at home.  I knew.  I was asking questions and reading or trying to read books.  I knew ABCs and counting and all that pretty well.  Before I went up there.  But they took me to school with them.  And so I was the only one that size.  But the teacher got kind of a kick out of it.  Yes, he was a man teacher.  And so then he called Nellie to come up to the site.  And he'd take me on his lap and ask me all these questions.  And I was so embarrassed because I thought I was quite big.  And I thought I should sit on a desk like this.  I thought I was a little...  I was just embarrassed that he had to take me on a lap like I was a baby.  I can remember that part of it.  But anyhow, I just read.  Oh, I didn't go more than a week or two.  I don't think the weather was bad.  They just took me with them once in a while.  But I didn't know what...  I didn't learn what school was all about.  Now, do you still sing songs, old time songs?  I'd love to.  But my voice was giving out on me.  I guess I'm getting old and don't like to admit it.  I don't know.  But I've seen those.  And, well, I've always...  Oh, of later years here, I've sang.  There's a bunch of us who would sing, you know,  maybe in a choir at the church.  But they don't really have a regular choir.  But once in a while somebody would stir up a choir  and we'd do some singing and blow over again for a while.  But I've sang specials the last several years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had camp meetings.  Did they have those around here at all in the summer?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, they have...  In the early days.  Not really what the camp meetings used to have in the East.  But they have had summer camp for the children.  And then, you know, their camp...  But not in their earliest days.  But they got so they did have them.  When my boys were small,  they'd take them up to...  Oh, up in the Coeur d'Alene area.  Long Lake or Rock Lake or someplace.  I forgot the name of it.  Rocky Point, I think it was called.  And they spent two weeks of vacation up there.  With the...  With the Christian training.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there church activity here when you first came in 1907?  No.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, there wasn't.  But it wasn't long before they decided,  well, there's enough people here, they ought to have a church.  They could...  See, there's enough of them to make it interesting.  So they did build this church up here.  What is now the Presbyterian Church.  They began to build that church.  And it was called the...  The Community Church.  And they figured, well, this...  Too small a town to segregate this little bunch and that little bunch.  All the different groups.  And so they...  It was just to be a community church.  And everybody that wanted to use it was free to use it.  And, well, I started in...  Then as soon as it started, the church started.  And I was very active in that.  I was their secretary, treasurer.  And then finally...  And I was teaching one of the smaller classes for a while.  And...  And I was busy with the ladies' aid.  You know, to the mother...  Like to...  But after a while, that kind of changed.  They began to get more people coming in.  And then there was quite a few Catholics had moved into town for that time, too.  And they...  I think they had the building, the time to for meetings.  They'd have to use...  Pick their days, you know, during the week.  They'd pick their day that they wanted to.  And so they didn't all have the same...  They had the same day.  And that stirred up a confusion between them.  And some didn't think that...  Well, their idea was that the Catholic religion wasn't according to the Bible.  Or according to God's teaching, as they figured it.  And that they shouldn't...  They shouldn't...  They thought they didn't have a church.  And they...  Well, the Catholics said...  Well, they donated on the...  The work that built the church.  And they thought they should have it, too.  And they...  Began to grow...  Kind of a...  Hard feelings between them and all that.  And finally they said, no, they weren't going to do more to the Catholic.  And they...  They kept on until they got a fix of it.  That they could...  Handle it through the presbytery.  And, well, the funds were not coming in too fast to keep it going.  And they thought, well, by turning it over to the presbytery management,  that they would draw some money from them.  From the group to help keep it going.  And that's...  It's turned out eventually to be a Presbyterian church.  What did the Catholics do when they couldn't use their church anymore?  Well, I don't know.  It seems to me that they met someplace else.  But they built a church of their own here.  In a short while.  I don't remember just how soon it was.  But they built their own church.  Just kind of across the street from the other church of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I imagine they must not have been very happy.  No, that...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I wasn't...  But there's a whole lot of people who weren't happy about it.  Because if that was built for a congregational church,  everybody's church,  well, everybody should have been able to use it.  I think.  Regardless of their denomination.  And...  If it had been decided in the first place,  if it was to be a Catholic church,  or if it was to be a Presbyterian church,  I think it should have been there.  But...  So the Catholics were singled out,  and all the different Protestant groups could use it.  Well, they...  I think they had some...  A few I didn't mind.  That if this one or that one wanted...  They wouldn't...  They wouldn't have nothing to have for certain meetings  or certain things that would come up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But mostly the Catholic religion.  Who made the decision about that?  Was it taken as a vote of the whole...  Of everybody that used the church?  Or were there certain people who had to...  Well...  Decision...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The power to decide?  I think it might have been more of that.  That the elders or whoever they were decided, I think.  I wouldn't say for sure, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, do you remember the railroads coming in here  and the ceremony that they had?  What's the story about that?  Well...

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: The...  Somebody had the picture of...  That I think...  To me, that was at the opening of this railroad...  Crowning, I guess you'd say.  When they ran a special, an excursion up from Potlatch.  And they picked up all the people in Potlatch and Princeton  and Palouse and around, you know, this railroad.  Started...  W-I-M went from Bovill to Palouse.  Anything along that line, you see, was W-I-M.  So they got an excursion up here and, boy, there was a mass of people here.  This excursion was something unusual at that time.  Up here, they didn't...  Wasn't having any train service or anything.  So everybody and his grandfather hopped on the train and came up that day.  It was all free.  And everybody was celebrating in general.  And I think that was for the finish of that railroad.  They opened it up for traffic for the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember anything about the celebration?  What went on?  Was it speeches or what was the deal about?  Yes, it was...  Whenever this was...  I think it was over the railroad.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I'm not too sure about Johnson's town at some time, but they did have...  And maybe that's...  That may be part of in that book, too.  It's mentioned in there, yeah.  But I think Mr. Laird and Deary and some of them, they've seen Deary's town.  They heard after little Deary.  And then...  Mr. Laird was a potlatch man.  He was part of the company.  And some of those fellows were speakers of the day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that embarrassment of one of the Bovill girls that John talks about  in the book?  She tried to break the...  Drive a spike or something like that.  And she couldn't do it.  He mentioned it.  No, I didn't.  She cried.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: She cried.  She cried.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She cried.  She was really embarrassed.  Everybody was looking at her and she was supposed to drive the spike or something like that.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, I didn't happen to remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any things, any scrapes that the Bovill girls got into or any adventures  that you all had when you were first here in those early days?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, I don't think I do.  Don't remember anything.  They were just common, nice, mild girls and I don't associate them with anything unusual  that I can think of.  And then as time went on, Mrs. Bovill, who did I say it was, she was a young woman.  She was the postmistress. She was the doctor, nurse, and consultant in general.  It wasn't one of these people that goes through their head just because of that.  She was just common, everyday people. She just happened to be in a place to run some of those  things or know about others that maybe the people didn't understand as well as she did.  But she wasn't snooty about it.  But when it came to the war, and well, when the war was over,  and there was so much shouting,  all they just...  It was like a hullabaloo, just like it was everywhere.  John Groh had...  He didn't have...  I guess he got this beer from the swill.  They had kegs of beer sitting on the street corners down there, and everybody...  And a big dipper in there, and everybody helped themselves.  All that sort of thing.  People got drunk that night.  They had to drink for years.  And everybody turned out and went down to the center, that little park that they had in the  center of the town.  They were all gathered down there and having a celebration.  And of course the men were all drinking their beer.  And...  Uh...  It's...  The...  John Groh, I think.  He said...  He furnished the beer.  He was the storekeeper here then, at that time.  And...  And...  And now he...  And...  So the...  And now the...  Mr. Tarbox was an engineer here on the old 23 engine.  And...  He...  He hadn't drank for years.  He had a big family here.  And grown up children too.  Some of them.  And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And...  Was this Odin Tarbox?  Mm-hmm.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah.  It was Odin.  And...  And he started from...  He was bringing the people in at the time of that fire, that forest fire.  I'm gonna ask you about that later.  That's a big story by itself.  What happened to him this night with John Groh?  Well, everybody was drinking so heavily.  Well, they...  They kept on...  Well, they got Tarbox and sidled off, you know, and they're all slapping each other on  the back in a ha-ha and around and around until they got Mr. Tarbox drunk.  And he was up to...  He had to drive that engine back, take those fellas back to camp, and to work.  When the time came, the camp was way up in here somewhere.  And when it got started by Mr. or the...  Someone said to I...  Well, Mrs. Tarbox said to him, but, oh, don't let him go out there and stoop like that with  the...  He don't know what he's doing.  And drive that engine.  I said, nothing.  And Walter, the older boy, said, no, Mom, let him go.  I said, he'll be all right, because I'll go out with him.  And he shared, nothing happens soon.  And so, Walter went out with him.  But partway out here, it was a bright moonlight night, you know.  And...  But...  He was heading along on his engine.  And all of a sudden, he grabs the controls somewhere and threw on the brake and just  jolted along.  And somebody said to him, what's the matter, Tarbox?  I said, don't you see that headlight coming there?  And he did.  It was so bright moonlight.  And he said, around the corner, he stared at it right in the face as he thought it was  the headlight of another engine.  And then...  And then...  And then...  And then it started.  And it's always been a joke with Tarbox trying to run from the moon.  He probably never lived that down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That sounds like it was really a big celebration that day.  Uh-huh.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And that was the day that Ernie got...  Well, no, that wasn't either...  No, there was another time...  When...  He got mixed up in the celebration.  But that was a fire. There'd been several fire issues, and those buildings burned down.  Some of them were in that time, and some of them two or three. Some of them cleaned up,  and some of those two men burned in the one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, before we get off the subject of the Bovill completely, you said that she was  a consultant, too. What kinds of things do you think that she would have information  about or could help people about?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, she was pretty well read on legal material, legal matters, and law procedure,  I think, and almost anything like that. She was a very studious woman. And, you know,  she was very good at that.  And she just visited time, educating herself. You know, but she didn't know, she was finding  out as fast as she could, I think. I think that had always been her way of doing.  And I don't know, did you hear anything about them coming from England and going to  there?  I mean, she was very hickey who was trying to say this because that direct was all obsessive  thinking about try and figure out a little bit better and could have, that sort of thing.  Imagine those little girls coming out of the dinghies so they didn't have no idea.  I mean, even though not all of them came there they still talked about Piglet and all  that stuff, that much.  But, then this way they came to consider that as a little girl because she didn't want  to talk very much about one of them about rules.  I mean, why should she talk about statistics coming in here, again.  She was a very strict person.  horses and she was quite a writer herself and they then finally they wound up down in  I think it was Colorado for more of their procedure and  so I don't know something was the point there I meant to tell you about that that's gone now  yeah it had to do with her learning things and things that she knew how to do  well yes she was in the school that first year of school yes she was she was one of the school  directors and so she every Friday afternoon we would have well sewing or they would  sometimes things like that to dress them  or whatever but you couldn't pray well with them either as most of the second year but  anyhow she'd read a story read a book and we could all listen to that and I know she read freckles  and some of the girl limberlost and some of those interesting stories limberlost the girl of the  limberlost and she would come to school and read them after recess she'd come in and then  she estaid herself well as if she had read a story she pod  . significant  earlier concrete there  No, it wasn't concrete at that time either. That was a wooden building, but it was later  a concrete building. In the afternoon, they had fixed it so that the, well, from the sixth  grade on up, I think, was the school, and then all the other little ones, and the other  school. She would come over to the schoolhouse on the Friday afternoons, and she would do  whatever project they had in mind then. But sometimes it was a slow process, and sometimes  it was sewing that they would do. They had some women around town that were good seamstresses,  and all that they did help with. It was kind of everybody's problem. I know the mother  was one of them that had to help them with the sewing, and the engineers and all. But  she was just extra good at sewing. Then there'd be, oh, I don't know other things that they  couldn't do, maybe, but they all had a project there to work on. At the other school, well,  there's a man teacher at the new school that they had. He had his youngsters, only quite  a few of them, I don't know, I guess a dozen and a half maybe or more. Anyhow, he had a  little bit of carpenter work.  Things like that. He was an engineer in that. They were building ironing boards and things  for their mother, learning how to use tools.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Besides sewing for the girls, was there other skills that they worked on in projects?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, I think, well, like something they could embroider or something like that, or  any kind of...  Yeah.  Fancy work or anything hand-work that they could do. I would scratch up if there was  something new I did every once in a while. Somebody would bring something that, you know,  I wanted to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Mrs. Bovill doing all these projects of hers, she didn't get paid for doing those

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: things?  No.  This was just service?  No, that's just service and gratis work. And then other people got, they were all pitched  in to help, you know.  And make something of the best of everything. And she did all, whatever she was doing. And  the mother and several others, you know, they donated their time and tools and teach them  sewing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, could Mrs. Bovill do a lot of this kind of thing, like nursing and all that,

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: because she had more free time and didn't have to do as much work as the other women?  Well, she didn't do it. She had a maid there at the house.  Oh, yeah.  And she had a maid there at the house. And she had a maid there at the house. And  most of her housework. And the girls had their certain jobs to do, you know. And that was  a little bit of help. And the hotel wasn't as big then as it is now. They added another  chunk onto it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she did have perhaps more free time and so on?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yeah, she was kind of, she kind of had to engineer and boss a lot of things that came  up. She had to be, you know, she had to be a good housekeeper.  Yeah.  And she had her own kitchen and other things that she could have instructed about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this maid full-time?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Yes. She lived there with them. They had her. And she was, well, she was cooked,  and, well, she was just general helper. And it was, it was Sophie Bird was her name.  But, as it doesn't seem very, well after a year or two, or two years, she got married  to the fellow that was working there.  the Spokane Hotel, Dave Ellison.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you figure that if you were going to say somebody was the community leader that  it would have been the Bovill in those real early days?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, yes, I think so.  She was just kind of a natural woman leader.  It was just natural for her to do those things.  Some people might know a lot, but they're too scared to say so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you still didn't feel at all that  that

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: that

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: they thought that they were superior to other people?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: No, no, they weren't that kind.  They were just here, roughing it like the rest of them were.  She happened to have what was needed here, and they didn't all have it.  What do you mean, what was needed?  Well, you know,  for the,  for the women that were working here,  they didn't have any of that work that she was doing.  You know, all those things that she helped out with and all,  and her nursing and helping with the sick and all.  She just happened to have that,  that knack,  and she kept hunting up something,  and every time a new project came along she was hunting it up in a,  in a book to find out all she could about it.  And,  and,  and,  and,  팔атур  er melhor  Duck  all  out  of  her  pad  Did you ever see the newspaper that had their entry here or their history of their time here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm not sure if I have or not. I may have, but I don't remember it. I've read so many things. I'm not sure if I've seen that or not.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: I have one somewhere. I saved it because it was linked right in with my early life here. I probably zip it now. It hasn't gotten lost.  Every time I think somebody knows, because the paper looks old and yellow and old, and I think, oh, there's nothing.  Some of those papers are just thrown away and just not around. Sometimes that happens.  Mm-hmm.  But much to my disgust.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I was going to ask you another thing about them. I've heard that in the summer, rich kids would come from the East or from other places and stay at the hotel as a summer place. Is that true? Have you heard that?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I don't know that... There was people who used to come.  Sometimes...  I think some were to do, maybe. People who lived there. Maybe some friends of the girls, maybe, who had been chums in their school in Spokane or something like that.  But there was... Well, they knew, the Bovills knew, so many people around Moscow, too, that they, because of these surveyors and...  Squatters, not squatters, but the locators, that they'd become acquainted with their families.  You know, sometimes after the man of the family in Moscow would come up this way and see them.  Or maybe the next time they'd come up, they'd bring up some of the family. They'd tell them to bring up this way.  So they'd bring up some of the family with them until they got acquainted and all.  And I guess there was times that I think there was a girl or two, you know, to come along and become acquainted with them and spend some time up here.  Of course, I wasn't around very much at that time. I wasn't up there. Or I was at school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they take a special pride in their horses here when you were here at the Bovills?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I've heard that they did. But I didn't know much about their horses.  The girls were awfully proud of their horses, and they loved to ride.  But I didn't know much about that because that was out of my line.  And it was a further...  Especially...  A little special little thing that came about sometimes.  Florence Smith was one of the early...  They were early families here.  And they just ran straight up the street in the next... the second block.  And...  Her father, his name was Jim. His name was Jim Smith.  But anyhow...  They had...  An old fellow, a sweet fellow that was...  Lived, batched I think, somewhere near them.  Anyhow, he left it up to this Mr. Smith or the family somewhere to look after his horse while he was gone.  And they could use it.  He was, I don't know, I guess out in the woods working or something.  And...  And how...  Florence...  Had to go up to...  To take the...  She wanted to catch the horse, or one thing.  And...  I don't know either whether she was going to take it.  I don't think she was going to take it back with her either.  She was just going to take it to feed, I guess was all.  Anyhow, she had a big tin can, you know, and a bucket thing that had been fixed.  And had that grain in it.  And she...  I don't know if I knew I'd ride up with her.  And...  So...  I...  I did.  And...  Now she took...  She took that horse up there.  And...  It's kind of so...  I don't remember it hardly.  But she...  It hit that horse.  It was to go up there and he was to feed it.  She was to take this feed for some other horses, I think.  That were...  And...  That was the way up.  And I guess your husband and I were, in other words...  Yes.  ...when I went out to이� wash on either of those hitchhiking trips and father and  spirit, I found myself sitting on the stars or something that we used to sit on theSt.  unexpected times.  And some of them, they couldn't find it, where they form on my little horse, except  to get in. I was riding behind her and I didn't know anything about horses. I was pretty scared  anyway. I had the, holding one arm around her and the other arm was my sack of feed,  or bucket of feed, and I tried to get him to go in there and he wouldn't go, the horse  wouldn't go in there and he balked. He stood there and she hollered at him and tried to  drive him and she was jerking him in the line probably and kind of kicking at him. He just  deliberately balked. He wasn't going anywhere.  I was trying to get him to go further and finally she, I think, let a yell out of her  and hollered at him and she could have gotten sore a little bit. She called him a nasty  name. He jumped right quick and went over that thing and we both figured it out. She  sat there and laughed. She said, it wasn't a girl that talked like that either, but if  she was so mad at him she could have killed him. When he jumped right ahead and surprised  us, it was a little bit of a surprise.  I hung on for dear life and when we got to where we were going and got him slowed down,  I still had, I don't think I lost a grain of that feed when I got there. She said, you  old fool, if I had known that's what you needed, I'd have called you that a long time ago.  She said, you said what?  I said, man, you don't even know me.  Because that's what he called you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You first met Ernie up on the homestead, you said. And you were only, you must have only

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: been about thirteen or something then. You were fourteen, you were still very young.  Well, more like fourteen, I guess, because I don't think he was that old.  I think we met him maybe the first year we went up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you just, did he continue to see you after you came to Bovill or were you just  acquaintances? Did he sort of have an eye out for you?  I kind of think he did. But, you see, after we gave up the homestead, you know, switched

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: to stone timber, we moved into town and of course that kind of, you know, one of us getting  into wintertime by that time anyhow. And the boys might not have been coming over so  often, but they'd come over every Sunday, a whole bunch of them. It was almost, I guess,  five or six or eight maybe of the men that we knew, you know, that the, one of the families  was staying with us. The man and his wife and two little kids and his sister Hollenbeck.  And, well, they were working with him.  And, you know, they were buying up there. And, but they gathered up the crew as they  came by. We went and we were always glad to see that much company and all of the kind  of break in the, in the road, road chain. And, so, so the, it was all this, Ora Hayes  and I think John McGarry was with them most of the time.  And, oh, a fellow, I can't think of his name now, but he was from Iowa, Des Moines, Iowa,  or, Des Moines, Iowa. And all the two brothers that are from the east, I think they were  from Missouri, they did, we got acquainted with them out here though. And, some, some  Steg boys with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you notice Ernie's standing out from the rest of them?

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: Well, I don't, they all kept kidding me about him and all.  About Ernie?  Yeah, they, they said, something about, he had his eye on you or something. And the folks  told, the folks would tell him, they said that he had, some of his folks had come out  here ahead of him. So, and they'd written back, they said, well, you folks go ahead  and tell them, we're not out of this country. So, they got a girl picked out of here, they'll  think it's just Sue Ernie or something like that. And, so, such things as that, they were,  was.  Do you think there was any truth in that or were they just pulling your leg?  Well, I thought they probably didn't know what they were talking about, but I guess  it did turn out that they did. But, you know, they, the boys would come over every Saturday  night. And, probably they'd come Saturday night and stay all night.  Really?  Yeah, they'd fix, they didn't have room, you know, for them. But, they'd fix the sleeping  place, you know, or covers out, or fix the spread covers on the floor. And, maybe they  wouldn't all get there at the same time, but most of the time they did. And we had a big  dinner the next day. And, they had raised everything in our garden out there. And, that's  different than batching.  Yeah.  And, the food they have when they're just batching.

words: Well, besides the meals, what would the visiting be like? Would they play games or what would  they do? Just sit and talk or what?  Yeah, it was both things. And, then some, they'd talk and they, sometimes I know they

words: used to get out, some of the boys get out there in the yard. We had the grassy yard  there, you know.  And, they'd get out there and get to, well, wrestling or, or, how did you call this?  They are wrestling.  Are wrestling. And, oh, any of those things, you know, just anything for entertainment.  And, it would finally get, it would get funny and they'd all start laughing. But.  How come they picked your place to come to?  Well, I guess, I don't know, I guess it must have been, maybe it's too much of a  bother for Mr. Holmendick or McGarry because Holmendick's family was there with us. And,  and, oh I know, we, some of them would come, sometimes too, I guess, and they got to play  with them, but they couldn't invite them all back whenever they, I never should have come.  Why, come on then, you know, we had lots of milk and cow there and lots of milk and lots  of cream and, you know.  That was pretty nice. She had her own vegetables and things in the garden. I thought that was pretty swell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you and Ernie actually start courting?

words: Oh, I don't know. We'd known each other for about five years. I guess he thought he'd claim me.  We always chatted together, you know. Whenever it was convenient or anything going on, he'd come and take me.  But I didn't consider it real seriously until about the end of it.  Five or six years, five.  He'd plan on it all of her own, I guess, at that time.  After we moved in town, you see, we went to shows all the time.  That's one thing. We started study. There's a movie picture show here, up at the old theater up there.  And we were...  We had always had a serial going.  And we could start on that, and he'd have to save that serial.  And the fellow who'd run the picture show was...  was a... took a fancy to Ernie. He was about all right.  And Ernie happened to... didn't get to go to that show that night.  He had to work, you know, on some engine or something. He had to work nights all the time.  And he found out...  And the man had run that show through the next night again so he'd see it.  So he'd already seen it, his show.  Mr. Anderson.

Uh-huh.  And so I was only about, about a month or so.  And I was, I was, uh, 15, 16 years old.  That's what I said.

words: Well, I never had any trouble like that, that I can recall, but it was, uh, well, I,  uh, I always, uh, asked if I could go someplace or told Mother where I was going, or, you know,  and I was, I guess I wasn't, they didn't have any trouble with me, because it wasn't hard  to manage, or anything.  I just was always, I grew up learning to mind.  And, that's all.  And I, I, my ideas were still, still there, she might say.  So did...

words: I was thinking with, with all the lumberjacks, all the, you know, drinking and all that  that was going on in town in the early days, whether they'd be worried about, you know...

words: Well, I didn't go, you see, I didn't go downtown.  Uh-huh.  I hardly, unless I'd just run down some groceries or something like that.  And, uh, but we didn't mix in much of that business.  And then the, the saloons were down there near the railroad track, right up there in the,  up behind where the stores are now.  And, uh, so, uh, they, they stayed pretty much to themselves.  But, I don't know.  Nowadays, it seemed like they would, they'd be, we had to mix in with the people.  But, we didn't have any trouble that way.  And then, there's about a few, we did, see, they were nice and polite and kind enough  to us.  And, uh, see, they didn't, they knew my folks.  And, they, they, I don't know, they were like that.  So, we...  But, I know one night we went to a dance here.  They, they didn't take us to the dance very much.  And, uh, Dad paid for the dance at night, so, mother and the kids, all of us, we went  up with the dance.  And one fellow asked me to take me home that night.  And I said, well, I'll see what the folks say about it.  I asked him if it was all right.  I didn't know what they were going to do.  But I hadn't been in any company with anybody anyhow,  and I didn't know where they wanted to go.  And I said, well, this guy wants to take me home.  I said, should I take me or should I not?  I didn't know what they wanted.  And I said, well, I guess it's all right.  Just walk along with the rest of us in the crowd, and it'll be all right.  It's all right.  He walked me home.  And I said, if you didn't come back in,  I don't think he would even expect to take the whole family.  So I didn't care.  I didn't care much about him anyhow.  He didn't have a very good reputation.  So I think later on, now there's another fellow in the town here  that is a real pal to all of us around.  And, well, the group that we kind of got mixed with  was kind of like home folks all along.  And...  Harry Jones was the one that, like me, I kind of fell for.  And his uncle, who was just pretty much the same age,  was Austin was his last name.  I don't remember what his name was.  But they would sometimes get us to go with the neighbors  to some kinds of parties.  You know?  And they'd all gang up and go up to us,  and pass up that way.  And we'd have a big video.  And maybe they'd push the table roll back.  And we'd play games, spin the pan, anything.  And post office.  And crazy things you could think of.  And then...  And then I went to honor walk us home.  And that's all he wants to do.  There's no foolishness.  You know?  Oh, they weren't the smoochy kind that you see nowadays so much.  You know?  Nowadays, you see, the guy that takes you home,  I guess they think they got a platter,  and they can fuss around over you.  And I wasn't used to that.  But anyhow.  And the next week or two,  we went to the New York High.  We went to some other place.  And the old folks, there was always the whole family,  babies, old folks, and all.  Young kids.  And that's where we had our fun.  Everybody was alive.  And everybody had fun.  And had a good big time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say they were like home folks,  you mean like the folks that the family left back in Missouri  when they first came out?  Is that what you mean?  Well, they...  Just like your own family.  Like, you know, families.

speaker: Nellie Wood Smith
words: And you didn't have to be, you know, sharing some of them  because they didn't quite suit your taste or anything.  They were just all alike and tried to be the same to each other.  And I know that one time, I got a surprise.  I was going with Perry quite a lot at that time.  And...  But they were just these regular parties.  And there wasn't any shows or anything.  And so we just all moved from place to place whenever the time came.  And we'd all get together.  And so I went.  It was Halloween.  And...  And I don't know to this day who did it.  But I guess my mother or some of Florence's...  Florence Smith is what I said rode the horse with me.  But she was going with this uncle, Walter.  Walter Austin was his name.  And I was going with Perry.  That is, going, I just...  He'd just come down sometimes and walk up with me to the place.  That's how much I was going with him.  But they were just...  It was fun to walk along.  And he wasn't mottling over you or anything.  He just felt free to just go along like it's your brother or anybody else.  You know.  You didn't have to worry about him.  And that's the way things were run then.  And anyhow...  We just had an awful lot of fun from just nothing.  And anyhow, we come Halloween time.  And we thought that we'd...  But mother knew of it because we were making a pretty basket.  You know, the boys were batching.  They were living in their aunt's house close to us.  And they were batching.  I didn't think they fared very well.  Maybe they were batching.  So Florence and I decided we'd fix up a nice basket.  And then they'd slip up there and hang that basket for him.  Because...  But we watched it.  So they were going downtown.  And they were going to slip up and hang up the basket.  Or leave it there at the door.  And so we went along.  And everything was dark.  But it had been all evening.  And we had to go in the outer door to this kind of back porch there.  And we closed it.  And the other door was just about four or five feet from it, I think.  And it got it all fixed.  And later it was in pretty good timing.  So we could just see where we were going.  And we slipped up there and put the basket, you know, right in front of the door where they'd have to run into it.  They were kind of whispering.  And then they'd sneak down that again.  They'd have to go on home and let them find it.  They'd probably know who did it.  But we wasn't going to.  And we got the sticks in the basket and the door.  And that door flew open.  And the boys were in there.  And they were like scared the wits out of us.  We started to run.  And it wasn't all clear in there then.  It was brush and stuff.  And they chased us for, I guess, fifteen minutes all over the beach.  And they could catch up with us.  And I don't know.  I guess they finally did catch us.  They kind of got run down.  And I said, well, you're on your knees.  You know you wasn't supposed to be at home.  Did you leave the baskets when you ran?  Yes.  We just fixed them.  You know, we had them sit down there.  And they started to turn away.  And then they...  And that's like they took a little bit of death.  Because they didn't take advantage that time.  They kissed us then.  And that's what they...  They had to fight so hard then before they got a kiss.  And you know, they kept the kisses.  And then all the things on the back of their neck.

words: Did your folks have a pretty high standard of, oh, did they,  like did they teach you to tell the truth and things like that?  Absolutely.

And I said, if you haven't done what's right, if it's your own wrong,  the best thing you can do is fix it up.  And I said, yeah.
